figures.caption: Figure 3: Correlation between the proposed discrepancy metric dNTP(f ;Z) and the test accuracy, measured on various task vector extraction methods f and the demonstrations Z. We test on four variants of f : Function Vector [41], Task Vector [16], State Vector [24], and I2CL [28], each of which is shown in different colors. Here, each point corresponds to the result for different demonstrations Z. Pearson correlation coefficients (ρ) are reported for each method f . Across four classification benchmarks (columns) and two models (rows), lower dNTP(f ;Z) consistently correlates with higher accuracy, validating our metric as a principled criterion for the quality of the task vector.

Table 1: Classification accuracy (%) of LTV and four TV methods, tested on eight benchmarks. We also compare with two inference modes (zero-shot and ICL) for reference. LTV method achieves the highest average accuracy (Avg.) across all models; check full results on five LLMs in Appendix A.7.

Table 5: Classification accuracy (%) of LTV when task vectors obtained from a large model are transferred and applied to a small model for task vector-based inference. We use Qwen-2.5-72B as the large model and Qwen-2.5-7B as the small model. As a reference, we report zero-shot, ICL, and LTV inference of each model using its own task vectors. Values in parentheses indicate the change relative to the LTV performance of the small model.

Table 6: Overview of the benchmark datasets used in our experiments, including their prompting templates, label sets, and licenses. {Sentence} and {Label} are placeholders for input query x and label y, respectively. All datasets are publicly available and used under their original licenses or standard research-use terms.

Table 7: Complete results for Table 1 in the main paper. We report the classification accuracy (%) of LTV and four baseline task vector methods on eight benchmarks across all five LLMs evaluated in this work. The accuracy of Zero-shot and ICL inference is provided as reference inference modes. LTV consistently achieves the highest average accuracy (Avg.) across all models.

Figure 6: Extraction time cost versus downstream accuracy of LTV and MLP-based mappings with varying depth. All methods are tested on LLaMA-3.1-8B, and accuracy is averaged over eight classification benchmarks used in the main paper in Sec. 6.

Figure 7: Extraction time cost versus downstream accuracy of LTV and PEFT-based methods (prompt tuning [23] and LoRA [18]), evaluated under three numbers of demonstrations (k = 10, 30, 50). All methods are tested on LLaMA-3.1-8B, and accuracy is averaged over eight classification benchmarks used in the main paper in Sec. 6.
